mappings.rationaleAI: Vertebrates first appeared in the Cambrian (B.1), jawed forms from Ordovician-Silurian (B.2-B.3), tetrapods from late Devonian (B.4), dinosaurs and mammals arose in the Triassic (C.1), birds from dinosaurs in the Jurassic (C.2), with diversification continuing through the Cenozoic (D).

Spans life history across all eras: Precambrian microbial evidence and Great Oxygenation Event (A), Cambrian explosion of animal phyla in the Paleozoic (B), successive mass extinctions through the Mesozoic (C), and mammalian diversification in the Cenozoic (D).

Geology as the science of Earth's processes over time provides the framework for understanding all eras and periods of geologic time, from Precambrian (A) through Paleozoic (B), Mesozoic (C), and Cenozoic (D).

4.54-billion-year history directly maps to all eras: Hadean and Archean in Precambrian (A), Cambrian explosion and Snowball Earth in Paleozoic (B), mass extinctions in Mesozoic (C), and mammalian diversification in Cenozoic (D).

Mammals diverged from reptiles in the Carboniferous (B.5), originated from cynodont therapsids in the Late Triassic (C.1), and diversified after non-avian dinosaur extinction at 66 Ma into the Cenozoic (D).

Animals originated in the late Cryogenian (~650 Mya) within Precambrian time (A), with the Cambrian explosion (~539 Mya) in the early Paleozoic (B.1), and continued diversification through the Mesozoic (C).

Evolution drives the change in heritable characteristics across all geologic eras, from the origin of life ~3.5-3.8 billion years ago in Precambrian (A) through speciation, adaptation, and extinction across the Paleozoic (B), Mesozoic (C), and Cenozoic (D).
Earth's origin ~4.5 billion years ago directly relevant to Precambrian time (A), with biological evolution spanning all subsequent eras.
Flowers evolved 150-190 million years ago in the Jurassic (C.2), enabling angiosperms to dominate most terrestrial ecosystems through the Cretaceous (C.3) and into the Cenozoic.
The origin of life ~3.5 billion years ago falls within Precambrian time (A), with subsequent evolution producing current biodiversity across all geological eras.
Paleoclimatology records climatic changes across geological time, particularly relevant to the Pleistocene Epoch (D.2.a) with its glaciation cycles and climate shifts.
Anatomically modern humans emerged ~300,000 years ago in the Quaternary Period (D.2), with earlier hominins spanning the Pleistocene Epoch (D.2.a).
The Stone Age spans from earliest Oldowan tools (~2.6 million years ago) through the Quaternary Period (D.2), encompassing Paleolithic through Neolithic within the Pleistocene and Holocene (D.2.a).
The period from first hominin stone-tool use (~3.3 million years ago) to invention of writing falls within the Quaternary Period (D.2), primarily the Pleistocene Epoch (D.2.a).
Homo sapiens evolved ~300,000 years ago, falling within the Quaternary Period (D.2) of the Cenozoic Era.
Horse evolution traceable from multi-toed Eohippus (45-55 million years ago) through the Tertiary Period (D.1) to domestication in the Quaternary (D.2).
Mass extinctions punctuate geological time, including the end-Permian event (B.6), Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction (C.3), and ongoing sixth mass extinction in the Cenozoic (D).
